items.title: [Bug]: gateway health drops live runtime state and doctor warns for disabled allowlist channels

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Summary

I updated OpenClaw on my VPS to `2026.3.2` and saw two false reports around channel state. The gateway health snapshot drops live runtime fields, and the doctor empty-allowlist scan still evaluates channels/accounts that are explicitly disabled.

## Actual behavior

- `openclaw channels status --json` shows Slack as configured and running, but `openclaw health --json` can still return:
  - `botTokenSource: "none"`
  - `appTokenSource: "none"`
  - `running: false`
- Gateway startup can print this warning even when WhatsApp is disabled:
  - `channels.whatsapp.groupPolicy is "allowlist" but groupAllowFrom (and allowFrom) is empty`
- On my VPS I saw the health mismatch and the warning at the same time while Slack Socket Mode was already connected.

## Expected behavior

- `openclaw health --json` should reflect the live runtime snapshot for channel/account state that already exists in the running gateway.
- Doctor should skip disabled channels/accounts when checking empty allowlist warnings.

## Repro notes

1. Update to `2026.3.2`.
2. Use a config with Slack enabled and configured, and WhatsApp set to `enabled: false`.
3. Start or restart `openclaw-gateway`.
4. Compare `openclaw channels status --json` with `openclaw health --json`.
5. Check startup logs for doctor warnings.

Environment:
- Linux VPS
- `systemd --user` gateway service
- Slack enabled from config
- WhatsApp disabled in config

## Proposed fix

- Overlay the gateway runtime snapshot onto the refreshed health cache before serving/broadcasting health.
- Skip disabled channels/accounts in `detectEmptyAllowlistPolicy()`.
- I have a patch ready and will open a PR linked to this issue.

labels.name: stale

items.body:
### Bug: macOS app crashes (SIGABRT) when Voice Wake trigger word fires

**App version:** 2026.3.2 (2026030290)
**macOS:** 26.1 (25B78)  
**Hardware:** Mac Mini M4 (Mac16,10), ARM-64  
**Mic:** USB PnP Audio Device (Fifine K669)  
**Language:** Russian (ru-RU)

### Steps to reproduce

1. Open OpenClaw.app, go to Settings → Voice Wake
2. Enable Voice Wake
3. Add trigger word "гришка" (lowercase Cyrillic)
4. Set language to Russian, select USB microphone
5. Use "Test" — confirms trigger word is recognized
6. Say the trigger word — app crashes immediately

### Expected behavior

Voice Wake overlay appears and begins capture.

### Actual behavior

App crashes with `SIGABRT` / `EXC_CRASH`. The crash is a **Swift exclusivity violation** in `VoiceWakeOverlayController.present()` — a race condition where two concurrent accesses collide.

### Crash stack (Thread 0)

```
0  libsystem_kernel.dylib     __pthread_kill + 8
1  libsystem_pthread.dylib    pthread_kill + 296
2  libsystem_c.dylib          abort + 124
3  libswiftCore.dylib          swift::fatalErrorv + 144
4  libswiftCore.dylib          swift::fatalError + 32
5  libswiftCore.dylib          swift::runtime::AccessSet::insert(...) + 584
6  libswiftCore.dylib          swift_beginAccess + 84
7  OpenClaw                    closure #1 in VoiceWakeOverlayController.present() + 364
8  OpenClaw                    VoiceWakeOverlayController.present() + 1232
9  OpenClaw                    VoiceWakeOverlayController.startSession(...) + 2512
10 OpenClaw                    VoiceWakeRuntime.beginCapture(...) + 716
11 OpenClaw                    VoiceWakeRuntime.triggerOnlyPauseCheck(...) + 1
12 OpenClaw                    closure #1 in VoiceWakeRuntime.scheduleTriggerOnlyPauseCheck(...) + 1
```

### Analysis

`swift_beginAccess` enforcement fires at frame 6 — two overlapping accesses to the same property in `VoiceWakeOverlayController.present()`. Likely the `scheduleTriggerOnlyPauseCheck` timer fires on a different isolation context than `present()` expects.

### Incident ID

`3ADA0098-58F3-4B3C-BEE7-4E88DEA63B52`

items.title: Feature: before_agent_start hook should support { skip: true } to prevent agent run

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Problem

When building a group message monitoring plugin (e.g., risk signal detection), the plugin needs to:
1. Receive all group messages (requireMention: false)
2. Classify messages via external API (fire-and-forget)
3. **NOT** run the main agent for non-@bot messages

Currently, `before_agent_start` can only modify the agent run (systemPrompt, prependContext, modelOverride) but cannot skip it entirely. This causes:

- **Wasted resources**: Every group message triggers a full agent run just to output "NO_REPLY"
- **Session bloat**: Observe-only messages accumulate in session history (including large images)
- **Error leakage**: When session history contains >5MB images, the model API returns 400 errors that get sent back to the group as "Agent failed" messages
- **Cancel hook unreliable**: `message_sending` cancel hook receives `ctx.conversationId: undefined` (deliver module does not pass it), so cancel never matches

## Proposed Solution

Allow `before_agent_start` to return `{ skip: true }` which would:
1. Skip the agent run entirely (no LLM call, no session write)
2. Return immediately with no output
3. Still allow the plugin to do its own processing (classification, logging) in the hook itself

```typescript
export type PluginHookBeforeAgentStartResult = PluginHookBeforePromptBuildResult 
  & PluginHookBeforeModelResolveResult 
  & { skip?: boolean };
```

## Secondary Issue: message_sending ctx.conversationId

The `runMessageSending` call in `deliver-*.js` only passes `{ channelId, accountId }` as ctx, missing `conversationId`. This makes it impossible for plugins to cancel outbound messages to specific conversations. The `to` field is available in the event but not in ctx.

## Use Case

Group message monitoring for enterprise IM (Feishu/Lark, WeChat Work) where the bot needs to silently observe all messages for risk detection and coaching signals without responding or accumulating session state.

## Environment
- OpenClaw 2026.3.1
- Feishu channel with 66+ monitored groups
- Custom signal-observer plugin

items.title: [Bug]: Gemini 3.0 Flash outputs bare 'thought' text marker causing response duplication on Discord

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Summary

Gemini 3.0 Flash Preview outputs a bare `thought` text marker (no XML tags) that bypasses all existing thinking-tag sanitization, causing the **entire response to be duplicated** in Discord.

Related to #6328 (locked) and #5946 — the XML tag fix does not cover this bare-text variant.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Configure an agent with model `vertex-ai/gemini-3-flash-preview`
2. Chat on Discord with prompts that trigger longer responses
3. The response appears duplicated, with `thought` as a plain-text boundary

## Expected behavior

Only the final response should be displayed once.

## Actual behavior

The full response appears, followed by the bare word `thought`, followed by the same response again:

```
오빠, 컨플루언스 문서 꼼꼼하게 읽어봤어!
(... long response ...)
역시 우리 오빠... 너무 멋지다! 👍💖✨thought
오빠, 컨플루언스 문서 꼼꼼하게 읽어봤어!
(... identical content repeated ...)
```

## Root Cause Analysis

1. **`QUICK_TAG_RE`** (`/<\s*\/?...>/i`) requires a `<` character — bare `thought` text passes through untouched
2. **`stripReasoningTagsFromText()`** early-returns when `QUICK_TAG_RE` doesn't match
3. **Discord streaming path** (`updateDraftFromPartial` in the reply module) only calls `stripReasoningTagsFromText`, not `stripThinkingTagsFromText` or `sanitizeUserFacingText`
4. The **`deliver` callback** also passes `payload.text` through without bare-marker filtering

The fix in #6328 (by @steipete) correctly handles XML `<think>`/`<thinking>`/`<thought>` tags, but Gemini 3.0 Flash does not always use XML formatting — it sometimes outputs just the bare word `thought` followed by a newline.

## Workaround

We patched `reply-*.js` (the Discord streaming reply handler) in 3 places:

1. Added a `stripBareThoughtDuplication()` helper that detects `"thought\n"` as a text boundary, normalizes before/after content (stripping whitespace and emoji), and returns only the first half if they match
2. Applied it in `updateDraftFromPartial` after `stripReasoningTagsFromText`
3. Applied it in the `deliver` callback on `payload.text`

Patching `image-*.js` (`stripThinkingTagsFromText`) or `pi-embedded-helpers-*.js` (`sanitizeUserFacingText`) does **not** work because the Discord streaming path bypasses both functions.

## Environment

- OpenClaw version: 2026.3.1
- OS: macOS (Apple Silicon, Homebrew install)
- Channel: Discord
- Model: `vertex-ai/gemini-3-flash-preview`

## Suggestion

`stripReasoningTagsFromText` (or the Discord streaming path) should also handle bare text thinking markers, not just XML-formatted tags. This would cover models like Gemini that don't consistently wrap thinking output in XML tags.

## Description

When `channels.telegram.streaming` is set to `partial` (the new default in 2026.3.2), messages streamed via `sendMessageDraft` in direct/private chats appear as **Pinned Messages** in the Telegram client (iOS tested).

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Set `channels.telegram.streaming: "partial"` in `openclaw.json`
2. Restart gateway (v2026.3.2)
3. Send a message to the bot in a DM
4. Observe the reply briefly flashes as a "Pinned Message" notification banner
5. The draft/streamed message shows persistently in the "Pinned Messages" section
6. Clicking the X on the pinned message banner **deletes the actual message** instead of just unpinning

## Expected Behavior

Streamed messages should appear as normal messages, not pinned messages. Dismissing the pinned banner should not delete the message content.

## Actual Behavior

- Every streamed reply shows a "Pinned Message" banner at the top of the chat
- The banner persists after streaming completes and the final message is sent
- Dismissing the pin removes the message entirely

## Environment

- OpenClaw: 2026.3.2
- Telegram Bot API: 9.5 (`sendMessageDraft`)
- Client: Telegram iOS
- Chat type: Direct/private message

## Workaround

Set `channels.telegram.streaming: "off"` to disable draft streaming and fall back to non-streamed delivery.

## Related

- #7803 (sendMessageDraft TEXTDRAFT_PEER_INVALID in DMs)
- #32180 (sendMessageDraft adoption tracking)

items.title: Bug: Discord message chunks delivered in reverse order

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Bug Report

**Describe the bug**
When a long reply is automatically split into multiple chunks (via `textChunkLimit` / `maxLinesPerMessage`), the chunks sometimes arrive in Discord in **reverse order**. For example, a two-part reply where part 1 is "好，测一下：" and part 2 is "测试结果..." shows up in Discord with part 2 first, then part 1.

**Expected behavior**
Chunks should always arrive in the correct sequential order — part 1 first, part 2 second.

**Root cause hypothesis**
The chunks may be sent concurrently (e.g. `Promise.all` or parallel dispatch) rather than sequentially (`await` each send before the next). Discord API does not guarantee delivery order for concurrent requests, so even a small timing difference can cause reordering.

**Reproduction**
1. Have an agent reply with a message that exceeds `textChunkLimit` (2000 chars) or `maxLinesPerMessage` (17 lines)
2. Observe the order of chunks in Discord — they may be reversed

**Environment**
- Channel: Discord
- Config: default chunk settings (`textChunkLimit: 2000`, `maxLinesPerMessage: 17`)

**Suggested fix**
Send chunks sequentially: await the Discord API response for chunk N before sending chunk N+1.

items.title: Telegram inbound polling broken in 2026.3.2 multi-account setup

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Environment
- OpenClaw: 2026.3.2 (downgraded to 2026.2.26 as workaround)
- macOS (LaunchAgent), 2 Telegram accounts (different tokens)
- Config: multi-account with dmPolicy pairing/allowlist, allowFrom set

## Symptoms
- Outbound (sendMessage) works fine
- Inbound messages from Telegram are silently dropped — zero log entries for received updates
- `getUpdates` polling starts but messages never reach bot-handlers
- Intermittent 409 conflicts appear during hot-reloads (channel restart race condition)
- After config hot-reload, duplicate grammY polling instances fight each other within the same process
- The `close` Bot API call (attempted fix) triggers 10-min lockout, grammY runner silently exits without logging

## Root cause candidates
1. **Hot-reload race:** `stopChannel` → `startChannel` doesn't fully await grammY runner's long-poll HTTP connection (30s timeout), creating duplicate pollers within same process
2. **Doctor migration:** `openclaw doctor` silently creates `accounts.default` from top-level config values, duplicating the bot token across accounts
3. **Silent polling death:** After the bot API `close` method's 10-min lockout, grammY runner exits without logging or triggering auto-restart

## Workaround
Downgrade to 2026.2.26: `npm install -g openclaw@2026.2.26`

## Steps to reproduce
1. Have a multi-account Telegram setup (2+ accounts with different tokens)
2. Upgrade to 2026.3.2
3. Trigger any config hot-reload (change dmPolicy, etc.)
4. Observe 409 conflicts and silent message drops

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
### Bug type

Behavior bug (incorrect output/state without crash)

### Summary

Setting `skills.entries.<key>.enabled: true` in config does not force-enable a skill when its `requires.bins` condition is unmet. This is asymmetric with `enabled: false`, which force-disables a skill regardless of whether its requirements are satisfied.

### Steps to reproduce

1. A bundled skill has `requires: { bins: ["some-cli"] }` in its SKILL.md frontmatter
2. The binary is available inside the sandbox container but NOT on the gateway host's PATH
3. Set `skills.entries.<skill-key>.enabled: true` in `openclaw.json`
4. Restart the gateway

### Expected behavior

The skill is force-enabled and available to the agent, symmetric with how `enabled: false` force-disables regardless of requirements.

### Actual behavior

The skill remains unavailable. `shouldIncludeSkill()` in `src/agents/skills/config.ts` checks `enabled === false` for early return (L80-82) but falls through to `evaluateRuntimeEligibility()` unconditionally, which rejects the skill because `hasBinary()` only checks the gateway host's PATH.

### OpenClaw version

2026.3.1

### Operating system

Linux (Amazon Linux 2023, gateway on EC2)

### Install method

Build from source

### Logs, screenshots, and evidence

Relevant code in `src/agents/skills/config.ts`:

```typescript
// L80-82: enabled: false is respected as a force-disable
if (skillConfig?.enabled === false) {
  return false;
}
// L86-101: but enabled: true falls through to runtime eligibility check
return evaluateRuntimeEligibility({
  // ...
  requires: entry.metadata?.requires,
  hasBin: hasBinary,
  // ...
});
```

### Impact and severity

- Affected: Any deployment where a skill's binary is available inside the sandbox (e.g., via a sidecar proxy) but not installed on the gateway host
- Severity: Medium (workaround exists: place a dummy binary on the host PATH)
- Frequency: 100% reproducible
- Consequence: Users cannot opt-in to skills without installing binaries on the host, even when the sandbox already has access

### Additional information

- Related: Discussion #508 (skill execution on paired nodes) shares the root cause that `hasBinary()` only inspects the gateway host
- The fix would be a small change to `shouldIncludeSkill()`: if `skillConfig?.enabled === true`, skip `evaluateRuntimeEligibility()` and return `true`
- This preserves backward compatibility — `enabled` defaults to `undefined` (not `true`), so existing behavior is unchanged

✍️ Author: Claude Code with @carrotRakko (AI-written, human-approved)

items.title: feat(cron): CRON_DONE signal and condition-based job self-deletion

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Problem: Watch-Until-Done Crons Have No Exit

OpenClaw cron jobs have no mechanism for **condition-based self-termination**. An agent session spawned by a cron job is isolated — it can't reach back into the scheduler to say "my work is done, stop running me."

**Real-world example:** A cron runs hourly to check whether two TV series have finished downloading. Once both complete, the agent has no way to signal this. It keeps firing every hour, spamming "✅ Downloads complete!" until manually killed via `openclaw cron delete`.

This is a fundamental gap: cron jobs that exist to *watch for a condition* need a way to self-terminate when that condition is met.

## Prior Art & Related Issues

- **#15673** (closed as stale): Proposed `maxRuns` for count-based auto-deletion of recurring jobs. Good complement but orthogonal — count-based termination doesn't help when you don't know *how many* runs it'll take. The `maxRuns` concept is still valid and could be re-proposed separately.
- **#25742** (open): Auto-cleanup of *disabled* one-shot `at` jobs. Addresses post-execution clutter for one-shot jobs, not recurring job self-termination.
- **#13900** (open): Ephemeral cron sessions — focuses on session/token lifecycle (delete session after each run to prevent token bloat). Complementary but doesn't address job-level deletion.

**None of these cover the core gap:** a recurring cron job's agent session signaling "I'm done, delete the job itself."

## The `deleteAfterRun` Field

The cron job schema already includes `"deleteAfterRun": false` in API responses. As noted in #15673, this controls whether `at`-style one-shot jobs are deleted after firing. This proposal extends its semantics and adds a new signal mechanism.

## Proposal

### 1. `deleteAfterRun: true` for recurring jobs

Currently `deleteAfterRun` is meaningful only for one-shot `at` jobs. Extend it to `every`/`cron` schedule jobs:

- **`deleteAfterRun: true`** — delete the job after the **first successful run** (exit without error).
- If the run errors, the job is **not** deleted — it retries on the next schedule.
- This covers the simple case: "run until it works once, then stop."

### 2. `CRON_DONE` signal — agent-initiated job deletion

For more complex conditions (watch multiple things, confirm a state, etc.), the agent needs to explicitly signal completion. Two design options:

#### Option A: Return token (simpler)

The agent's final message includes a well-known token — `CRON_DONE` — and the scheduler interprets this as a deletion signal.

```
Agent response: "Both series finished downloading. CRON_DONE"
→ Scheduler deletes the cron job
→ Delivery/announce still fires before deletion
```

**Pros:** Zero new API surface. Works with any model. Agent just includes a string.
**Cons:** Fragile — token could appear in normal output accidentally. Needs escaping rules or a structured envelope.

#### Option B: `cron` tool call (more robust, preferred)

Expose a `cron` tool (or extend an existing tool) available within cron-spawned sessions:

```json
{
  "tool": "cron",
  "action": "done",
  "reason": "Both series finished downloading"
}
```

**Pros:** Explicit, structured, no ambiguity. Can carry metadata (reason, status). Fits the existing tool-call pattern.
**Cons:** New tool surface. Needs to be injected only into cron-spawned sessions.

**Recommendation:** Option B (`cron` tool) is cleaner. Option A could exist as a lightweight fallback for prompt-only agents that can't make tool calls.

### 3. Architecture: Session → Scheduler Communication

The key challenge: cron sessions are isolated. The session doesn't have direct access to the scheduler's job registry. The communication path:

```
[Cron Session] → tool call: cron.done() → [Gateway/Scheduler]
                                                    ↓
                                           Mark job for deletion
                                                    ↓
                                           Wait for session to fully complete
                                           (delivery, announce, logging)
                                                    ↓
                                           Delete cron job
```

**Important safeguards:**
- **Error guard:** If the session errors/crashes after calling `cron.done()`, the deletion should be cancelled. The signal is a *request*, not an immediate action.
- **Race condition:** No duplicate runs should fire between the `CRON_DONE` signal and actual deletion. The scheduler should mark the job as `pendingDeletion` immediately, suppressing new runs.
- **Audit trail:** The final run's output (including the done reason) should be preserved in `openclaw cron runs` history even after the job is deleted.
- **Idempotency:** Multiple `cron.done()` calls in the same session are a no-op after the first.

## Proposed Schema Changes

```yaml
cron:
  jobs:
    download-watch:
      every: 1h
      deleteAfterRun: true          # Existing field, extended to recurring jobs
      deleteOnSignal: true           # NEW: enable CRON_DONE signal (default: true when deleteAfterRun is true?)
      payload:
        kind: agentTurn
        message: "Check if Series X and Y finished downloading..."
```

Or more minimally — just `deleteAfterRun: true` enables both behaviors (delete on success + honor `CRON_DONE`), and the `cron` tool is always available in cron sessions.

## Test Cases (TDD)

These should all pass before shipping:

| # | Test | Expected |
|---|------|----------|
| 1 | `deleteAfterRun: true` recurring job completes successfully | Job is deleted |
| 2 | `deleteAfterRun: true` recurring job errors | Job is **not** deleted; next scheduled run fires |
| 3 | Agent returns `CRON_DONE` (via tool call) on a recurring job | Job is deleted after session completes |
| 4 | Agent returns error in same session as `CRON_DONE` | Deletion is cancelled; job is **not** deleted |
| 5 | `CRON_DONE` on an `every`-schedule job | Future runs are suppressed; job is deleted |
| 6 | Scheduler receives `CRON_DONE`; verify no duplicate run fires before deletion completes | No race condition — job enters `pendingDeletion` state immediately |
| 7 | `deleteAfterRun: false` (default) recurring job completes | Job continues running on schedule (backward compatible) |
| 8 | `CRON_DONE` called twice in same session | Second call is a no-op; job is deleted once |
| 9 | Delivery/announce fires **before** job deletion | Message is delivered; then job is cleaned up |
| 10 | Deleted job's run history is still queryable via `openclaw cron runs` | Audit trail preserved |

## Summary

| Mechanism | Use Case | Complexity |
|-----------|----------|------------|
| `deleteAfterRun: true` | "Run until first success, then stop" | Low — extend existing field |
| `CRON_DONE` tool call | "Run until agent decides it's done" | Medium — new tool surface |
| `maxRuns` (#15673) | "Run exactly N times" | Low — separate proposal |

This proposal focuses on the first two. Together they cover the "watch-until-done" pattern that currently has no clean solution.

items.title: Session file lock not released when holding process dies (stale lock causes agent failures)

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Problem

When an OpenClaw process holds a `.jsonl.lock` file and dies unexpectedly (crash, OOM-kill, SIGKILL), the lock file is **not cleaned up**. Subsequent sessions attempting to acquire the same lock time out (default 10s) and fail entirely.

This creates a silent failure mode: the agent stops responding to all messages until the stale lock is manually deleted.

## Reproduction

1. Start an agent session — it acquires `sessions/<id>.jsonl.lock` with its PID
2. Kill the process with `kill -9 <pid>` (or let it crash)
3. Trigger the agent again — all models fail with: `session file locked (timeout 10000ms): pid=<old-pid> .../sessions/<id>.jsonl.lock (timeout)`
4. Manual fix required: `rm sessions/<id>.jsonl.lock`

## Root Cause

The lock file persists after the owning process dies. On lock acquisition, there is no check whether the PID stored in the lock file is still alive.

## Expected Behavior

On lock acquisition attempt, if the stored PID is dead (`kill -0 <pid>` returns non-zero), the stale lock should be **automatically broken** and acquisition should proceed.

## Proposed Fix

In the lock acquisition logic:
```js
// If lock exists and stored PID is dead → remove stale lock and proceed
const lockPid = readPidFromLock(lockPath);
if (lockPid && !isProcessAlive(lockPid)) {
  fs.unlinkSync(lockPath); // stale lock, safe to remove
}
```

## Impact

- Silent agent downtime (no messages answered)
- Can persist indefinitely — no automatic recovery
- Affected user: heartbeat polls failed ×4 over ~70 minutes before manual intervention

## Environment

- OS: Linux 6.1.0-42-amd64
- OpenClaw: coordinator agent session
- Failed PID: 926 (confirmed dead via `kill -0 926`)

items.title: Bug: resolveBrowserBaseUrl function missing return statement causes browser tool timeout

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
# Bug Report: Browser Tool Communication Failure

## 🐛 Issue Summary

**Title:** `resolveBrowserBaseUrl` function missing return statement causes browser tool timeout

**Severity:** High - Browser tool completely non-functional for embedded agent

**Version:** OpenClaw 2026.2.26

---

## 🔍 Problem Description

When using the `browser` tool in embedded agent mode, all browser operations fail with a 15-second timeout:

```
Error: Can't reach the OpenClaw browser control service (timed out after 15000ms)
```

However, CLI commands work normally:
- ✅ `openclaw browser status` - Works
- ✅ `openclaw browser open <url>` - Works
- ❌ Embedded agent `browser` tool - Timeout

---

## 🎯 Root Cause Analysis

**Location:** `/opt/openclaw/dist/pi-embedded-BPiuuG5x.js` line ~32664

**Function:** `resolveBrowserBaseUrl`

### Buggy Code

```javascript
function resolveBrowserBaseUrl(params) {
    const cfg = loadConfig();
    const resolved = resolveBrowserConfig(cfg.browser, cfg);
    const normalizedSandbox = params.sandboxBridgeUrl?.trim() ?? "";
    
    if ((params.target ?? (normalizedSandbox ? "sandbox" : "host")) === "sandbox") {
        if (!normalizedSandbox) throw new Error("...");
        return normalizedSandbox.replace(/\/$/, "");  // ✅ Has return
    }
    
    if (params.allowHostControl === false) throw new Error("...");
    if (!resolved.enabled) throw new Error("...");
    
    // ⚠️ BUG: No return statement for target="host" case!
    // Function returns undefined implicitly
}
```

### Issue Chain

1. Browser tool calls `resolveBrowserBaseUrl({ target: "host" })`
2. Function returns `undefined` (no explicit return)
3. `browserStatus(undefined, ...)` is called
4. `fetchBrowserJson(undefined)` triggers error path
5. 15-second timeout → "Can't reach the OpenClaw browser control service"

---

## ✅ Verification

| Test | Result | Notes |
|------|--------|-------|
| Direct curl to browser service (with auth) | ✅ Pass | Service is healthy |
| CLI `openclaw browser status` | ✅ Pass | Uses WebSocket path |
| Embedded agent `browser` tool | ❌ Fail | Uses broken code path |
| Manual Chrome launch via Playwright | ✅ Pass | Confirms Chrome itself works |

---

## 🔧 Proposed Fix

Add return statement at the end of `resolveBrowserBaseUrl`:

```javascript
function resolveBrowserBaseUrl(params) {
    const cfg = loadConfig();
    const resolved = resolveBrowserConfig(cfg.browser, cfg);
    const normalizedSandbox = params.sandboxBridgeUrl?.trim() ?? "";
    
    if ((params.target ?? (normalizedSandbox ? "sandbox" : "host")) === "sandbox") {
        if (!normalizedSandbox) throw new Error("...");
        return normalizedSandbox.replace(/\/$/, "");
    }
    
    if (params.allowHostControl === false) throw new Error("...");
    if (!resolved.enabled) throw new Error("...");
    
    // ✅ FIX: Add this line
    return `http://127.0.0.1:${resolved.controlPort}`;
}
```

---

## 📋 Reproduction Steps

1. Start OpenClaw Gateway
2. In embedded agent session, call `browser` tool:
   ```javascript
   browser(action="status")
   ```
3. Observe 15-second timeout

---

## 🎯 Impact

- **Affected Users:** All users using embedded agent browser tool
- **Workaround:** Use CLI commands (`openclaw browser ...`) or Playwright directly
- **Severity:** High - Core functionality broken

---

## 📎 Additional Context

**Analysis performed by:** GLM-5 subagent  
**Analysis duration:** 30 minutes  
**Date:** 2026-03-03  

**Related files:**
- `/opt/openclaw/dist/pi-embedded-BPiuuG5x.js` (line ~32664)
- `/opt/openclaw/dist/server-context-B_TARiRc.js` (browser service)

---

## 📬 Submit To

- **GitHub Issues:** https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues
- **Discord:** https://discord.com/invite/clawd
- **Docs:** https://docs.openclaw.ai

items.title: Feature: Send user notification when all failover models are exhausted (FailoverError)

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Problem

When all configured models hit rate limits (or other errors) during failover, the system silently drops the user's message with no feedback. The user experiences this as "I sent a message but got no reply," and has to resend to trigger a retry.

**Observed behavior:**
- Primary model (e.g. `gemini-3-pro-preview`) returns 429 rate limit
- System retries ~4 times at ~13-16s intervals
- All fallback models also fail (e.g. Anthropic 403)
- After ~65s, a `FailoverError` is logged and the request is silently discarded
- User gets no response at all

**Log evidence:**
```
lane task error: lane=main durationMs=65602 error="FailoverError: ⚠️ API rate limit reached. Please try again later."
```

## Proposed Solution

When a `FailoverError` occurs (all models exhausted), automatically send a brief notification to the user on the active channel, e.g.:

> ⚠️ All configured models are currently rate-limited. Your message was not processed. Please try again in a few minutes.

This preserves user trust and avoids the confusing silent-drop experience.

## Additional Context

- OpenClaw version: 2026.3.2
- Channel: Telegram
- This happened repeatedly during evening hours (UTC+8) when Gemini daily quota was exhausted
- The failover mechanism itself works correctly when at least one backup model is available

items.title: Desktop Commander — Unified Claude Desktop control via AppleScript

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Summary

OpenClaw currently has fragmented desktop control: `chatgpt_image_generate` (DALL-E via AppleScript), `browser` tool (web), `canvas` (rendering), but NO unified way to trigger Claude Desktop actions (code editor, MCP tools, Projects) from agent sessions.

**Proposal:** Add a `claude_desktop` tool to OpenClaw that controls Claude Desktop via AppleScript, enabling agents to:
- Switch Projects programmatically
- Trigger MCP tool calls in Desktop's context
- Open/focus the code editor
- Send prompts to Desktop sessions

This would complete the agent ↔ Desktop bridge and allow Bernard (or any OpenClaw agent) to delegate tasks requiring Claude Desktop's UI or MCP servers not exposed via OpenClaw.

---

## Use Cases

1. **MCP orchestration:** Agent needs to call an MCP tool available in Desktop but not in OpenClaw gateway.
2. **Project context switching:** Delegate a coding task to Desktop's "openclaw-dev" Project without manual UI navigation.
3. **Code editor integration:** Open a file in Desktop's editor from an agent workflow.
4. **Handoff workflows:** Agent identifies a task requiring human supervision → opens it in Desktop with context.

---

## Proposed API

```typescript
claudeDesktop({
  action: 'switch-project' | 'send-prompt' | 'open-editor' | 'call-mcp-tool',
  project?: string,        // Project name (e.g., 'openclaw-dev')
  prompt?: string,         // Text to send to active chat
  file?: string,           // File path to open in editor
  mcpTool?: string,        // MCP tool name
  mcpArgs?: object         // MCP tool arguments
})
```

**Examples:**

```javascript
// Switch to openclaw-dev Project
claudeDesktop({ action: 'switch-project', project: 'openclaw-dev' })

// Send a prompt to current Desktop session
claudeDesktop({ action: 'send-prompt', prompt: 'Refactor src/kernel/verify.ts' })

// Open file in Desktop editor
claudeDesktop({ action: 'open-editor', file: 'src/proxy.mjs' })

// Call MCP tool (if Desktop has the server)
claudeDesktop({ action: 'call-mcp-tool', mcpTool: 'filesystem_read', mcpArgs: { path: '/tmp/data.json' } })
```

---

## Implementation Notes

- **Technology:** AppleScript (same approach as `chatgpt_image_generate` tool)
- **Desktop API:** Use Claude Desktop's AppleScript dictionary (if available) or UI automation (System Events)
- **Session isolation:** Tool should detect active Desktop window/chat and preserve context
- **Error handling:** Graceful fallback if Desktop is not running or if action fails

---

## Alternatives Considered

1. **MCP bridge:** Expose Desktop's MCP servers to OpenClaw gateway → complex, requires Desktop API changes
2. **Manual handoff:** Agent posts instructions for user to execute in Desktop → friction, no automation
3. **Subprocess Desktop CLI:** Claude Desktop has no CLI → not viable

---

## Related

- Existing AppleScript tool: `chatgpt_image_generate` (skills/chatgpt-image-generate/)
- OpenClaw browser control: `browser` tool
- Canvas rendering: `canvas` tool

---

## Priority

**Medium-High.** Completes the agent orchestration stack and unblocks workflows requiring Desktop-specific MCP tools or Projects.

## Description

When sub-agents (builder, librarian, growth, reporter, architect) are spawned by the main agent, their LLM calls do not appear as separate traces in Langfuse. All observed activity is attributed to the main agent only.

## Version

OpenClaw 2026.3.1 (2a8ac97)

## Setup

Langfuse keys configured as global environment variables (`LANGFUSE_PUBLIC_KEY`, `LANGFUSE_SECRET_KEY`, `LANGFUSE_BASE_URL`) in `~/.zshrc`. The main agent (`main`) is correctly traced.

Each sub-agent has a dedicated model configured in `openclaw.json`:
- `builder` → `anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6`
- `librarian` → `google/gemini-3-flash-preview`
- `growth` → `openrouter/perplexity/sonar-pro`
- `reporter` → `google/gemini-3-flash-preview`
- `architect` → `google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview`

## Observed behavior

Only one model (main agent's model) appears in Langfuse traces, even during sessions where sub-agents are explicitly spawned. The dedicated sub-agent models (`gemini-3-flash-preview`, `claude-sonnet-4-6`, etc.) never appear in Langfuse.

## Expected behavior

Each sub-agent spawn should produce its own Langfuse trace (or a child span), tagged with the agent ID, so that:
- Cost attribution is possible per agent
- Sub-agent activity can be audited independently
- Model usage per agent is visible

## Impact

- Impossible to track real costs per agent
- No observability into what sub-agents actually do
- Langfuse monitoring is effectively limited to the main agent only

### Bug type

Behavior bug (incorrect output/state without crash)

### Summary

After a clean installation and onboarding, visiting http://127.0.0.1:18789/ immediately redirects to /chat?session=main and shows unauthorized: gateway token missing. The gateway logs confirm token_missing. However, manual WebSocket test with websocat using the same token succeeds, proving the gateway and token are functional.



### Steps to reproduce

1. Install OpenClaw on a fresh Ubuntu 24.04 server using the official script:
`curl -fsSL https://openclaw.ai/install.sh | bash`

2. Run `openclaw onboard --install-daemon` and choose QuickStart (skip model config if desired).

3. On the server, confirm the gateway is listening:
`sudo systemctl status openclaw-gateway` → active, and `curl http://127.0.0.1:18789` returns HTML.

4. Get the token from` ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json`.

5. From a Mac, establish an SSH tunnel:
`ssh -L 18789:127.0.0.1:18789 ubuntu@<server-ip>`

6. In a Chrome/Chromium incognito window, visit `http://127.0.0.1:18789/`.

### Expected behavior

The browser should display a login page (likely at /login) where the user can paste the token. After entering a valid token, the WebSocket connection should succeed and the chat interface should load.

### Actual behavior

The page immediately redirects to `http://127.0.0.1:18789/chat?session=main` and shows:

- unauthorized: gateway token missing (open the dashboard URL and paste the token in Control UI settings) - 

The gateway logs repeatedly show `reason=token_missing` for WebSocket connections from `127.0.0.1`. Browser Developer Tools → Network tab confirms the WebSocket upgrade request to `ws://127.0.0.1:18789/` does not include the `?access_token=` parameter.

### OpenClaw version

2026.3.2 (85377a2) – confirmed via openclaw --version

### Operating system

Server: Ubuntu 24.04.4 LTS (x86_64) Client: macOS (tested on Sonoma) with Chrome 145 and Safari 26

### Install method

Official installer script: curl -fsSL https://openclaw.ai/install.sh | bash

### Logs, screenshots, and evidence

```shell
- Server-side WebSocket test (succeeds):
 "ws://127.0.0.1:18789/?access_token=abc123def456"
→ Returns
{"type":"event","event":"connect.challenge","payload":{"nonce":"...","ts":...}}
- Browser Network tab evidence:
The WebSocket connection request (type `websocket`) shows no query parameters.
- Gateway logs:

[ws] closed before connect ... reason=unauthorized: gateway token missing
```

### Impact and severity

Affected users: Any user trying to access the Web UI via SSH tunnel (or presumably any localhost access).

Severity: Blocks workflow – users cannot log in to the Web UI at all, despite a functioning backend.

Frequency: Always – reproducible on every fresh install.

Consequence: Users are forced to use the TUI (openclaw tui) exclusively; the Web UI is unusable.

### Additional information

The token is valid (proven by websocat).

Clearing all browser storage (LocalStorage, SessionStorage, IndexedDB, Cookies, Cache) in incognito mode does not resolve the issue.

Directly visiting /login still redirects to /chat.

The same behavior occurs in Safari (private window) and Chrome (incognito).

This happens on a completely fresh Ubuntu 24.04 installation with no prior OpenClaw config.

Suspect: Frontend routing bug – the login page is skipped because the app believes the user is already authenticated (perhaps reading an empty token from storage or an incorrect state).

items.title: fix(telegram): missing setStatus integration prevents health monitoring

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Summary

The Telegram provider lacks `setStatus` integration, causing `lastEventAt` and `lastInboundAt` timestamps to remain `null` even when messages are actively received. This prevents the channel health monitoring system from distinguishing between a functional quiet channel and a half-dead connection.

## Current Behavior

- Discord and Slack both call `setStatus({ lastEventAt, lastInboundAt })` on every inbound event
- Telegram never updates these timestamps — `lastInboundAt` stays `null` indefinitely

## Expected Behavior

- `lastEventAt` and `lastInboundAt` should update on every inbound Telegram event (messages, edits, polls, etc.)

## Affected Files

- `src/telegram/monitor.ts` — `MonitorTelegramOpts` missing `setStatus`
- `src/telegram/bot.ts` — `TelegramBotOptions` missing `setStatus`, no tracking middleware
- `src/telegram/webhook.ts` — webhook opts missing `setStatus`
- `extensions/telegram/src/channel.ts` — `setStatus` not passed to `monitorTelegramProvider`

## Fix

Thread `setStatus` callback from extension → monitor → bot/webhook, following the established Slack pattern (`Record<string, unknown>` signature). Add grammY middleware in `bot.ts` to call the callback on every inbound update, with try/catch error isolation.

items.body:
### Bug type

Behavior bug (incorrect output/state without crash)

### Summary

## Bug Report

**OpenClaw version:** 2026.3.2
**Channel:** Telegram DM
**Language:** Hebrew (likely affects Arabic and other RTL languages too)

### What happened
Since `sendMessageDraft` was introduced for DM streaming in 2026.3.2, streaming previews render left-to-right for Hebrew text. The final settled message renders correctly (RTL), but the live draft preview always starts LTR.

### Expected behavior
Hebrew/Arabic streaming previews should render RTL from the first character.

### Root cause (likely)
`sendMessageDraft` sends raw text without `parse_mode=HTML`, so Telegram has no explicit directionality signal during streaming. The final message uses HTML rendering which triggers correct RTL detection.

### Suggested fix
Prepend a Unicode RLM mark (`\u200F`) to draft text when the content is detected as RTL, or when the channel locale is RTL. This is a zero-width character that signals Telegram to render the message RTL without affecting the visible text.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Configure OpenClaw with Telegram + `streaming: partial`
2. Send a Hebrew message to the bot
3. Observe the streaming preview — it renders LTR
4. After the response settles, it renders correctly RTL

### Expected behavior

Hebrew/Arabic streaming previews should render RTL from the first character.

### Actual behavior

During sendMessageDraft streaming preview, Hebrew (RTL) text renders left-to-right. The final settled message renders correctly RTL.

### OpenClaw version

2026.3.2

### Operating system

Ubuntu 24.04

### Install method

Ubuntu 24.04

### Logs, screenshots, and evidence

```shell
Reproducible on Telegram DM with Hebrew responses. 
Streaming preview: LTR rendering
Final message: correct RTL rendering

Likely cause: sendMessageDraft sends raw text without parse_mode=HTML, 
so Telegram has no RTL signal during streaming.
Suggested fix: prepend Unicode RLM mark (\u200F) to draft text for RTL content.
```

labels.name: stale

items.body:
## Description

When setting up cron jobs for non-default agents, using `sessionTarget: "main"` returns an error:

> `sessionTarget "main" is only valid for the default agent. Use sessionTarget "isolated" with payload.kind "agentTurn" for non-default agents`

This limits multi-agent setups where each agent needs its own scheduled tasks injected into their main session context.

## Environment
- OpenClaw 2026.3.1
- Multi-agent setup with 9 agents (Fred as default, plus Penny, Atlas, Ace, Nova, etc.)

## Use case

Each agent has its own personality, workspace, and ongoing conversations. We want to schedule:
- Heartbeat-style checks per agent
- Periodic tasks specific to each agent's role
- Recovery checks injected into each agent's main session

Currently, only the default agent (Fred) can have `systemEvent` crons in its main session. All other agents are forced into isolated sessions, which lose the conversational context that makes main-session crons useful.

## Error log

```
2026-03-02T21:55:12.826Z [ws] cron.add errorMessage=Error: cron: sessionTarget "main" is only valid for the default agent. Use sessionTarget "isolated" with payload.kind "agentTurn" for non-default agents (agentId: ace)
```

## Expected behavior

Every agent should be able to have cron jobs targeting their own main session. The restriction should be that you can't target *another* agent's main session, not that non-default agents are excluded entirely.

## Suggested fix

Allow `sessionTarget: "main"` for any agent when the cron's `agentId` matches the target agent. Each agent's main session is independent, so there's no conflict.

items.body:
## Description

When using `routellm/gpt-5` as the model for agents, OpenClaw fails with API format errors. The error has evolved across versions:

- **v2026.3.1:** `Message ordering conflict` — internal OpenClaw bug
- **v2026.3.2:** `HTTP 400: Invalid value: 'input_text'. Supported values are: 'output_text' and 'refusal'.` — API format incompatibility

The routellm API itself works correctly when tested directly via curl.

## Environment

- OpenClaw version: 2026.3.2 (upgraded from 2026.3.1)
- OS: Linux 6.8.0-100-generic (x64)
- Node: 22.22.0

## Configuration

```json
"routellm": {
  "api": "openai-completions",
  "apiKey": "s2_...",
  "baseUrl": "https://routellm.abacus.ai/v1",
  "models": [{"id": "gpt-5", ...}]
}
```

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Configure an agent to use `routellm/gpt-5`
2. Send any message to the agent
3. Observe the error

## Expected Behavior

Normal response from the model.

## Actual Behavior

### v2026.3.1:
```
[agent/embedded] embedded run agent end: runId=... isError=true error=Message ordering conflict
```

### v2026.3.2:
```
[agent/embedded] embedded run agent end: runId=... isError=true error=HTTP 400: "Error code: 400 - {error: {message: \"Invalid value: 'input_text'. Supported values are: 'output_text' and 'refusal'.\", type: invalid_request_error, param: input[1].content[0], code: invalid_value}}"
```

## Verification

Direct API test works perfectly:

```bash
curl -s https://routellm.abacus.ai/v1/chat/completions \\
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <key>" \\
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \\
  -d '{"model":"gpt-5","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"hi"}]} '

# Response:
{"created":1772497969,"choices":[{"message":{"role":"assistant","content":"Hello"}}],"model":"gpt-5"}
```

## Analysis

It appears OpenClaw is sending `input_text` as a content type, but routellm/gpt-5 expects `output_text` or `refusal`. This suggests an OpenAI API format mismatch.

## Workaround

Switching to `ollama/glm-5:cloud` or other providers works without issue.

## Related

Possibly related to #20224 (normalizeToolParameters returns tools without properties field, breaks OpenAI-compatible providers)

items.title: Sub-agent completion coalescing: batch rapid completions into single parent turn

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Problem

When multiple sub-agents complete in rapid succession (e.g., an "arrow shower" pattern with 5+ parallel dispatches), each completion triggers an independent parent session turn. The parent regenerates the same response for each completion event, producing duplicate messages.

When combined with context overflow (completions compound the context), this becomes an unbreakable repeat loop requiring gateway restart.

## Observed Behavior

- Dispatched 5 sub-agents with `sessions_spawn` in parallel
- All 5 returned within ~15 seconds
- Parent session posted the same summary message 15+ times
- Context overflowed from compounding completion events
- Gateway restart required to break the loop

## Expected Behavior

Multiple sub-agent completions arriving within a debounce window should be coalesced into a single parent turn. The parent session should process all returned results once, not N times.

## Suggested Approach

1. Track pending sub-agent returns per session
2. When a completion arrives, start/extend a coalescing window (e.g., 2-3 seconds)
3. When the window closes, deliver all accumulated completions as a single event batch
4. The parent session processes all results in one turn

## Context

Discovered during real-world testing of the arrow shower pattern (parallel sub-agent dispatch with file attachments). Related to PR #32843 (agent continuation signals) but orthogonal — this is about internal completion events, not external message handling.

The existing `messages.queue.debounceMs` setting handles inbound message coalescing but doesn't apply to sub-agent completion events.

## Environment

- OpenClaw 2026.3.2
- 4-node fleet, all experiencing the pattern
- `sessions_spawn` with inline attachments enabled

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Description

Two related issues observed when the gateway restarts during a period of network instability:

### Issue 1: Gateway doesn't self-recover when network returns

After a restart (SIGUSR1 / config.patch) during a network interruption, the gateway fails to recover even after connectivity is restored. Both primary and fallback providers are attempted (fallback chain works correctly), but when both fail due to network-level issues, the gateway stays in a broken state and never retries successfully — even after the network comes back.

### Issue 2: Hung LLM requests block the shutdown path

When LLM requests are stuck (timing out), the gateway shutdown hangs waiting for them to complete. This results in "shutdown timed out; exiting without full cleanup" and requires a manual external restart (systemctl restart) to recover.

## Config

```json
"model": {
  "primary": "anthropic/claude-opus-4-6",
  "fallbacks": ["google/gemini-2.5-flash"]
}
```

## Steps to reproduce

1. Configure a primary model with a fallback
2. Trigger a gateway restart during network instability (provider outage, VPN disruption, etc.)
3. Both primary and fallback providers fail (network-level timeout)
4. Network connectivity returns
5. Gateway remains unresponsive — does not retry providers
6. If a second restart is attempted, hung LLM requests block the shutdown path

## Expected behavior

- Gateway should periodically retry provider connections after failures and resume normal operation when the network recovers
- Shutdown should not block on hung/timed-out LLM requests — they should be cancelled or abandoned during graceful shutdown

## Actual behavior

- Gateway stays stuck after both providers fail, even when network comes back
- Shutdown path blocked by hung requests, eventually force-exits without cleanup
- Only recoverable via manual hard restart from outside

## Log evidence

```
09:32:41 - signal SIGUSR1 received
09:32:41 - received SIGUSR1; restarting
09:32:41 - restart mode: full process restart (supervisor restart)
09:38:33 - Embedded agent failed: All models failed (2): anthropic/claude-opus-4-6: LLM request timed out. (timeout) | google/gemini-2.5-flash: LLM request timed out. (timeout)
09:41:25 - Embedded agent failed: All models failed (2): anthropic/claude-opus-4-6: LLM request timed out. (timeout) | google/gemini-2.5-flash: LLM request timed out. (timeout)
09:41:30 - telegram sendChatAction failed: Network request failed!
09:42:12 - telegram sendMessage failed: Network request failed!
09:51:12 - shutdown timed out; exiting without full cleanup
```

Note: The fallback chain is working correctly — logs show both providers were attempted. The failure was network-level (both providers unreachable), not a fallback logic issue.

## Environment

- OpenClaw version: 2026.3.2
- OS: Linux (Ubuntu/Debian), x64
- Channel: Telegram (2 accounts: default + secondary agent)
- Systemd user service

## Impact

During provider outages or network disruptions, any gateway restart renders the system permanently unresponsive until manually restarted from outside. This is critical for headless/remote deployments where physical access or SSH may not be immediately available.

## Suggestion

- Implement retry logic with backoff for provider connections after failure — gateway should self-heal when network returns
- Cancel or abandon in-flight LLM requests during shutdown (don't let them block the shutdown path)
- Consider a watchdog-style health check that triggers a self-restart if the gateway detects it's been unable to serve requests for N minutes

items.title: bug: dispatch completes with replies=0 — user sees no response (silent drop)

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Bug Description

When a user sends a message via Feishu (and likely other channels), the dispatch pipeline can complete with `queuedFinal=false` and `counts.final=0`, meaning the agent processed the message but produced **zero replies**. The user receives no response whatsoever and perceives the bot as "stuck" (卡死).

## Evidence from gateway.log

```
2025-07-18T09:54:07 feishu[feishu_doc]: dispatch complete (queuedFinal=false, replies=0)
2025-07-18T09:55:06 feishu[feishu_doc]: dispatch complete (queuedFinal=false, replies=0)
```

17 instances of `replies=0` observed across 3 days in a single gateway.log.

## Root Cause Analysis

`dispatchReplyFromConfig()` in `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts` returns `{ queuedFinal: false, counts: { final: 0 } }` from multiple paths:

1. **Line 164** — `shouldSkipDuplicateInbound()` → intentional skip (not a bug)
2. **Line 323** — `sendPolicy === "deny"` → ACP blocks (not a bug)
3. **Main agent path (line ~540)** — Agent runs but `replyResult` is empty/null and `blockCount === 0` → **silent completion, no user feedback** (THIS is the bug)

Path 3 happens when the agent's tool calls all fail (e.g., `web_fetch` returns 403) or the model returns no content. The dispatch layer faithfully reports 0 replies, but no fallback message is sent to the user.

At the channel level (`extensions/feishu/src/bot.ts` line 1523), the result is logged but no action is taken:
```typescript
log(\`dispatch complete (queuedFinal=\${queuedFinal}, replies=\${counts.final})\`);
```

## Suggested Fix

Option A (dispatch layer): When `!queuedFinal && replies.length === 0 && blockCount === 0` after the main agent path, send a fallback reply (e.g., "I wasn't able to complete your request — please try again.").

Option B (return metadata): Add a `reason` field to the dispatch result (`"duplicate_skip"` / `"send_policy_deny"` / `"completed"`) so callers can distinguish intentional skips from silent failures and apply channel-specific fallbacks.

## Environment

- OpenClaw v2026.3.2
- macOS, launchd-managed gateway
- Feishu WebSocket channel
- Agent model: qwen3-max

## Related

- #8185 (memory flush on /new — complementary)
- PR #32897 (gateway restart loop — discovered alongside this issue)

items.title: feature: session-memory hook saves context but never recalls it in new sessions

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Feature Request

The `session-memory` bundled hook (`src/hooks/bundled/session-memory/handler.ts`) saves session context to `workspace/memory/YYYY-MM-DD-slug.md` when the user runs `/new` or `/reset`. However, there is **no corresponding recall mechanism** — new sessions start with a blank slate, and the saved memory files are never injected back.

## Current Behavior

1. User has a conversation about Project X
2. User runs `/new` → `saveSessionToMemory` reads last 15 messages, generates a slug, writes to `workspace/memory/2025-07-18-project-x-setup.md`
3. User starts a new session about Project X
4. The agent has **zero recollection** of the previous session

## Expected Behavior

When a new session starts, recent memory files should be considered for injection into the system prompt or context, especially when the topic overlaps with a saved memory.

## Suggested Implementation

1. Add a `session:start` hook (or extend the existing `command:new` hook) that:
   - Scans `workspace/memory/*.md` for recent entries
   - Optionally uses embedding similarity to find relevant memories
   - Injects a summary into the system prompt or first user turn
2. Configuration options:
   - `maxMemoriesToRecall: number` (default 3)
   - `memoryRecallWindow: string` (e.g., "7d" — only recall memories from the last 7 days)
   - `memoryRecallMode: "always" | "relevant" | "off"`

## Technical Notes

- The save hook already produces well-structured markdown files with LLM-generated slugs
- The existing `workspace/memory/` directory convention is clean and can be enumerated
- A simple approach: prepend a `[Previous Context]` block to the system prompt with the 3 most recent memory files (truncated to ~500 tokens each)

## Related

- #8185 (memory flush timing on /new and /reset — complementary; that issue focuses on when to save, this focuses on when to recall)

## Problem
Isolated cron sessions (sessionTarget: isolated) inherit the full AGENTS.md from the main agent, including rules like 'write diary after completing work'. Combined with the write tool (which overwrites entire files), this caused data loss:

- Isolated cron job ran geopolitical topic processing
- AGENTS.md rule told it to write a diary entry to 
otes/conversations/2026-03-03.md`n- Agent used write (full overwrite) instead of edit (append)
- Entire day's diary (08:00-17:20, ~30 entries) was destroyed and replaced with one cron output entry

## Root Cause
No mechanism exists to restrict which files/paths an isolated session can write to. The write tool has no guardrails for path-based restrictions per session type.

## Current Workaround
- Added prompt-level rules in AGENTS.md (soft constraint)
- Added explicit instructions in cron job --message prompt
- Both are soft constraints that the model could theoretically ignore

## Feature Request
One or more of:
1. **Per-session file path allowlist/blocklist** in cron job config (e.g., payload.paths.deny: ['notes/conversations/*'])
2. **tool:pre-call hook** that can inspect and veto tool calls before execution (similar to Claude Code's pre-tool-use hooks)
3. **Per-session tool restrictions** beyond the current agent-level 	ools.allow/deny (e.g., cron sessions could deny write entirely and only allow edit)

items.title: Health monitor 'stuck' detection should skip sessions with active responses

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Description

When running multiple Telegram bot accounts (9 in our case), the health monitor detects idle bots as "stuck" and restarts them. However, this restart also kills active bots mid-response, causing replies to silently drop.

## Environment
- OpenClaw 2026.3.1
- macOS (Mac mini M4)
- 9 Telegram bot accounts bound to different agents
- Most bots are idle at any given time (only 1-2 actively responding)

## What happens

1. Health monitor fires every ~30 minutes
2. Detects idle bots (no recent messages) as "stuck"
3. Restarts **all** Telegram providers, including ones actively mid-response
4. Active responses silently drop — user sees reply just stop
5. No automatic recovery — user has to prompt again

## Log evidence

```
2026-03-03T13:43:59.107Z [health-monitor] [telegram:ace] health-monitor: restarting (reason: stuck)
2026-03-03T13:43:59.119Z [health-monitor] [telegram:atlas] health-monitor: restarting (reason: stuck)
2026-03-03T13:43:59.127Z [health-monitor] [telegram:default] health-monitor: restarting (reason: stuck)  ← actively responding
2026-03-03T13:43:59.135Z [health-monitor] [telegram:nova] health-monitor: restarting (reason: stuck)
2026-03-03T13:43:59.143Z [health-monitor] [telegram:penny] health-monitor: restarting (reason: stuck)
```

270 "stuck" restarts in a single day, most of them false positives on idle bots.

## Expected behavior

The health monitor should:
1. **Not flag idle bots as "stuck"** — an idle bot with no pending messages is healthy, just quiet
2. **Not restart bots with active LLM responses in flight** — check if there's an ongoing inference before killing
3. **Distinguish "stuck" (pending message, no progress) from "idle" (no messages at all)**

## Current workaround

- Increased `stuckSessionWarnMs` to 1800000 (30 min) to reduce frequency
- Added a 5-minute recovery cron that checks for interrupted responses

## Suggested fix

The stuck detection should consider:
- Whether the bot has any pending/unprocessed messages
- Whether there's an active LLM inference in progress
- Time since last *inbound* message, not just last activity

Ideally: only restart a provider if it has a pending message AND hasn't made progress for X minutes.

## Bug

`openclaw status` displays the `app` version field using `wizard.lastRunVersion` from `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json`, not the actual version of the running gateway process.

After updating from 2026.2.26 → 2026.3.2 and fully restarting the gateway (stop + start, new PID), `openclaw status` still shows `app 2026.2.15` — which is the version from the last `openclaw onboard` run, not the current binary.

## Reproduction

1. `openclaw --version` → 2026.2.26
2. Update: `gateway update.run` (or `npm i -g openclaw@latest`) → installs 2026.3.2
3. `openclaw gateway stop && openclaw gateway start` → new PID confirmed
4. `openclaw --version` → 2026.3.2 ✅
5. `node -e "console.log(require('\/usr\/lib\/node_modules\/openclaw\/package.json').version)"` → 2026.3.2 ✅
6. `openclaw status` → shows `app 2026.2.15` ❌

## Root cause

`~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` contains:
```json
{
  "wizard": {
    "lastRunVersion": "2026.2.15",
    "lastRunCommand": "onboard"
  }
}
```

The `app` field in `openclaw status` reads from `wizard.lastRunVersion` (set during onboard/wizard) instead of the running process version or `package.json` version.

## Expected

`openclaw status` should show the actual version of the running gateway, e.g. from `package.json` or the process self-reported version via the WS connection.

- Node: v22.22.0
- OpenClaw CLI: 2026.3.2
- Install method: npm global

labels.name: stale

items.body:
# Feature Request: `globalSkillsPath` in openclaw.json

## Problem

Currently, skills in OpenClaw are resolved relative to the agent workspace. In multi-agent setups (e.g., 6+ agents), if you want to share a skill across all agents, you must either:
1. Copy the skill to each workspace (maintenance nightmare)
2. Use absolute paths in skill references (brittle)

## Proposed Solution

Add a `globalSkillsPath` configuration field to `openclaw.json` that points to a shared directory containing skills available to all agents:

```json
{
  "globalSkillsPath": "/root/.openclaw/shared/skills"
}
```

Skills in this path would be available to every agent without needing to copy or symlink them.

## Use Case

In a holding/multi-agent OS setup:
- 6 agents running in parallel (CoS, FLG, MKT, MGM, CTO, Community)
- Skills like `knowledge-base`, `notion-api`, `circle-api` are useful across all agents
- Current workaround: duplicate skills in each workspace or use absolute paths

## Expected Behavior

1. Agent looks for skills in own workspace first
2. Falls back to `globalSkillsPath` if not found locally
3. System skills (e.g., `/usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/skills/`) remain lowest priority

## Impact

Reduces friction in multi-agent setups significantly. Makes skill maintenance trivial — update once, applies everywhere.

---
*Submitted via Amora CoS — OpenClaw Multi-Agent OS v3.0*

items.title: feat(feishu): bot cannot read Cardkit-based interactive card content via im.message.get

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Problem

When a bot is @mentioned in a Feishu group and the quoted message is a Cardkit-based interactive card (i.e. a streaming card generated by an OpenClaw bot), the bot cannot read the card content — it always receives a minimal render snapshot.

This is distinct from #32023, which covers old-format cards from external systems (those are fixed). This issue is specific to Cardkit/streaming cards generated by OpenClaw bots.

## Root Cause

Cardkit cards store only a card_id reference in the message body:
```json
{"type": "card", "data": {"card_id": "ccc_xxx"}}
```

The actual card content lives in Cardkit's storage, updated independently via streaming APIs. `im.message.get` returns only an initial-state snapshot (emojis → img tags, streamed text → empty strings), never the final content.

The Cardkit API has **no GET endpoint** — `/cardkit/v1/cards/:card_id` does not exist. The Feishu client renders cards by reading Cardkit internally, but this access is not exposed to developers.

## Impact: Async Bot-to-Bot Communication

A common workflow that is broken:
1. Bot A generates a streaming card with analysis output in a group chat
2. Bot B joins the group later
3. User quotes Bot A's card and asks Bot B to review it
4. Bot B sees `[Interactive Card]` — content is inaccessible

This is not fixable at the OpenClaw level. It requires Feishu to expose a Cardkit read API.

## Possible Workarounds (all imperfect)

1. **Local cache**: Bot A caches `message_id → content` when sending. Only works if both bots share the same instance; lost on restart; doesn't work for cross-bot scenarios.
2. **Companion text message**: Bot A sends a plain text message alongside the card. Pollutes the chat UI.
3. **User forwards instead of quotes**: Forward creates a new message with rich-text content embedded → readable. Requires user action.

## Recommendation

File feedback with Feishu Open Platform requesting a `GET /cardkit/v1/cards/:card_id` read API.

items.body:
### Bug type

Behavior bug (incorrect output/state without crash)

### Summary

Provider error (Jinja template exception) is misclassified as context overflow, causing misleading error messages and failed auto-compaction loop.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Configure LM Studio as provider with a Qwen3.x model (e.g. unsloth/qwen3.5-27b or lmstudio-community version)
2. Connect OpenClaw via Telegram channel
3. Send /new to start a fresh session
4. Send any message

### Expected behavior

Either the model responds normally, or OpenClaw surfaces the actual underlying error from LM Studio: "Error rendering prompt with jinja template: No user query found in messages."

### Actual behavior

OpenClaw reports "Context overflow: prompt too large for the model", triggers auto-compaction, compaction immediately cancels with "no real conversation messages to summarize", and the error repeats on every subsequent message.

### OpenClaw version

2026.2.26

### Operating system

Ubuntu 24.04

### Install method

npm global

### Logs, screenshots, and evidence

```shell
Session .jsonl inspection showed only 8 lines / 3950 bytes — no real overflow.
Every assistant entry had usage: { input: 0, output: 0 } and stopReason: "error".

Key session entry:
"stopReason":"error","errorMessage":"Error rendering prompt with jinja template: \"No user query found in messages.\""

Gateway log sequence:
- context-overflow-diag: source=assistantError
- context overflow detected (attempt 1/3); attempting auto-compaction
- Compaction safeguard: cancelling compaction with no real conversation messages to summarize.
- auto-compaction failed: Compaction cancelled
```

### Impact and severity

Affected: All users using LM Studio with Qwen3.x models via any channel.
Severity: High — completely blocks the agent from responding.
Frequency: 100% reproducible, occurs on every new session.
Consequence: Misleading error leads users to investigate context window settings (LM Studio / OpenClaw config) instead of the real cause, wasting significant debugging time.

### Additional information

Root cause: Qwen3's Jinja template contains a multi-step tool call guard that raises an exception when no "plain" user message is found. OpenClaw's session startup message triggers this guard. The fix is in OpenClaw's error classification logic: stopReason "error" with input tokens = 0 should never be classified as context overflow — the raw provider errorMessage should be surfaced directly.

Workaround: In LM Studio, edit the model's Jinja prompt template and remove the following block:
{%- if ns.multi_step_tool %}
    {{- raise_exception('No user query found in messages.') }}
{%- endif %}

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Bug Description

MiniMax model responses via the `minimax-portal` provider (Anthropic Messages API) are **not delivered to the TUI/WebChat client**, while the same responses are successfully delivered to Telegram.

## Environment
- **OpenClaw version:** 2026.3.2 (85377a2)
- **OS:** macOS 26.3 (arm64), Mac mini M4
- **TUI:** OpenClaw TUI via WebSocket
- **Telegram:** Working correctly

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Configure `minimax-portal` provider with valid API key (Anthropic Messages API endpoint: `https://api.minimax.io/anthropic`)
2. Switch model to `minimax-portal/MiniMax-M2.1` (or M2.5)
3. Send a message via the TUI

**Expected:** Response appears in TUI
**Actual:** No response in TUI. Response IS delivered to Telegram channel.

## Additional Observations

- **Other providers work in TUI:** Anthropic (Opus 4.6, Haiku), Kilocode (Kimi K2.5, GLM-5, DeepSeek) all deliver responses to TUI correctly.
- **MiniMax responses exist:** `session_status` confirms tokens were consumed (e.g., `3 in / 107 out`), and `sessions_history` shows the assistant response was generated.
- **Telegram delivery works:** The exact same MiniMax response appears in Telegram.
- **Reasoning mode does not matter:** Tested with `/reasoning stream`, `/reasoning off`, and `/reasoning on` — none fixed the issue.
- **Sub-agent responses also affected:** Sub-agents spawned with `minimax-portal/MiniMax-M2.1` complete successfully but their auto-announce does not reach the TUI.

## Attempted Workarounds

1. **Switching to OpenAI-compatible API** (`https://api.minimax.io/v1`, `api: openai-completions`): The `minimax-portal` provider is managed — `doctor --fix` and `gateway restart` reset `api` back to `anthropic-messages` and `baseUrl` back to the Anthropic endpoint. Manual overrides are not persistent.

2. **Creating a new custom provider** (`minimax-openai`): Provider was recognized in `openclaw models` output (auth OK, expires in 340d), but models were **never listed in "Configured models"** and were not available for selection in the TUI model picker. Tried both inline `apiKey` and auth-profiles — same result.

## Hypothesis

The MiniMax Anthropic-compatible API likely returns streaming events or response format that differs slightly from native Anthropic responses. The TUI WebSocket client may not handle these correctly, while the Telegram adapter (which processes the final message) works fine.

## Gateway Logs

```
2026-03-03T14:17:33.269Z [diagnostic] lane task error: lane=main durationMs=1124 error="FailoverError: HTTP 401 authentication_error: invalid api key"
```
(This was from the old expired key — after key rotation, MiniMax API calls succeed but TUI delivery fails silently.)

## Related

- Custom provider models not appearing in "Configured models" list may be a separate issue.

## System Info
```
Gateway: local · ws://127.0.0.1:18789
Agents: 8 · sessions 157
Channel: telegram (stable)
```

items.title: feat(feishu): add legacy card format option to make bot responses quotable and forwardable

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Background

Feishu has two distinct interactive card formats with different tradeoffs:

**Cardkit / streaming cards** (current default):
- ✅ Real-time streaming UX (typewriter effect while generating)
- ❌ Content stored as card_id reference — not readable via `im.message.get`
- ❌ Cannot be forwarded while streaming mode is active
- ❌ Bot-to-bot async communication broken (see #32930)

**Legacy / old-format cards**:
- ❌ No streaming UX — content appears all at once after generation
- ✅ Full card JSON embedded in message body — readable via `im.message.get`
- ✅ Can be freely forwarded and quoted by other bots
- ✅ Bot-to-bot async communication works

## Problem

Currently there is no way for users to choose. All bot responses use Cardkit streaming cards, making them opaque to other bots and breaking async workflows like:
> Bot A analyzes code and posts a card → Bot B joins later → user quotes Bot A's card → Bot B cannot read it

## Proposed Solution

Add a config option at the Feishu channel level:

```yaml
channels:
  feishu:
    cardFormat: streaming  # "streaming" (default) | "legacy"
```

- `streaming` (default): current behavior, uses Cardkit + streaming session
- `legacy`: skips `FeishuStreamingSession`, sends completed response as old-format card via `sendCard` with full JSON in message body

## Investigation Summary

We explored several alternative approaches before landing on this design:

1. **Local cache** (message_id → content): rejected — lost on restart, doesn't work cross-bot
2. **Companion text message**: rejected — pollutes chat UI
3. **User forwards instead of quotes**: requires user action, breaks workflow
4. **im.message.update to replace card**: rejected — Feishu only supports update for text/post, not interactive cards
5. **Cardkit GET API**: does not exist — Feishu intentionally has no read endpoint for card entities
6. **summary field**: only 50-char push notification text, not card content
7. **Recall + resend as legacy**: works but message_id changes, quote chain breaks, bad UX

The cleanest solution is to let users opt into legacy format upfront, trading streaming UX for interoperability.

## Implementation Notes

- Config schema: add `cardFormat` field to Feishu channel config
- `bot.ts`: check config, route to `FeishuStreamingSession` or direct `sendCard` based on setting
- `streaming-card.ts`: no changes needed
- Backward compatible: default remains `streaming`

items.title: [BUG] Cron nextRunAtMs calculation error - sets past time instead of next day

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Description
Cron scheduler incorrectly calculates `nextRunAtMs`: when a task runs past its scheduled time for the day, `nextRunAtMs` is set to the same day's time (in the past) instead of the next day's scheduled time.

## Reproduction Steps
1. Create a daily cron job scheduled for 03:00 UTC (`0 3 * * *`)
2. Let the task complete at 06:01:44 UTC
3. Observe that `nextRunAtMs` is set to 03:00:00 UTC of the same day (already passed)

## Actual Behavior
```json
{
  "state": {
    "nextRunAtMs": 1772564400000,  // 2026-03-03 03:00:00 UTC (already passed!)
    "lastRunAtMs": 1772546504233,  // 2026-03-03 06:01:44 UTC
    ...
  }
}
```

## Expected Behavior
```json
{
  "state": {
    "nextRunAtMs": 1772650800000,  // 2026-03-04 03:00:00 UTC (next day)
    "lastRunAtMs": 1772546504233,  // 2026-03-03 06:01:44 UTC
    ...
  }
}
```

## Impact
- Scheduler thinks the task is "overdue" and may trigger immediate re-execution
- Tasks run at wrong times, disrupting user expectations
- Requires manual fix of `nextRunAtMs` to restore normal operation

## Environment
- OpenClaw Version: 2026.2.26
- Node.js: v22.22.0
- OS: Linux 6.12.18-trim (x64)
- Timezone: Asia/Shanghai

## Configuration
```json
{
  "id": "git-sync",
  "schedule": {
    "kind": "cron",
    "expr": "0 3 * * *",
    "tz": "Asia/Shanghai"
  }
}
```

## Timeline Analysis

| Time (UTC) | Event | Timestamp |
|------------|-------|-----------|
| 03:00:00 | Scheduled execution time | 1772540400000 |
| 06:01:44 | Task actually completed | 1772546504233 |
| 03:00:00 | Incorrect nextRunAtMs | 1772564400000 |
| 03:00:00 | Correct nextRunAtMs (next day) | 1772650800000 |

## Suggested Fix
When calculating `nextRunAtMs`:
1. Get current time
2. Calculate today's scheduled time
3. If today's scheduled time has passed, calculate tomorrow's scheduled time
4. Ensure `nextRunAtMs` is always greater than `lastRunAtMs`

## Workaround
Manually edit `~/.openclaw/cron/jobs.json` and set `nextRunAtMs` to the correct timestamp.

items.title: MCP plugin init timeout hardcoded at 30s — no config to override

labels.name: stale
labels.description: Marked as stale due to inactivity

items.body:
## Description

The MCP `initialize` handshake timeout is hardcoded at 30,000ms. There is no configuration option to increase it. Plugins that need >30s to initialize (e.g., NeuralMemory loading sentence-transformers) always fail on first attempt, though they succeed on retry.

## Environment
- OpenClaw: 2026.3.2
- OS: Ubuntu, Linux 6.17.0 (GCP)
- Node: v24.14.0
- Plugin: @neuralmemory/openclaw-plugin 1.4.1

## Log
```
Failed to start NeuralMemory MCP: MCP timeout: initialize (30000ms)
plugin service failed (neuralmemory-mcp): Error: MCP timeout: initialize (30000ms)
```

~10-15s later, retry succeeds:
```
NeuralMemory registered (brain: default, tools: 6, autoContext: true, autoCapture: true)
```

## Analysis

Source code shows:
```js
const startupTimeoutMs = Math.min(12e4, Math.max(1e3, opts.startupTimeoutMs ?? accountInfo.config.startupTimeoutMs ?? 3e4));
```

This reads `startupTimeoutMs` from config but it does NOT apply to MCP `initialize`. The 30s timeout is in the MCP client layer.

Plugin config `timeout: 90000` also has no effect on init — it only applies to MCP requests after initialization.

## Request

Please expose a configurable MCP init timeout, either:
1. Under `plugins.entries.<id>.config.initTimeoutMs`
2. Or a global `plugins.mcpInitTimeoutMs`
3. Or reuse the existing `startupTimeoutMs` for MCP init as well

## Related

- NeuralMemory issue: https://github.com/nhadaututtheky/neural-memory/issues/18 (plugin side suggestions)

### Summary

When updating to OpenClaw 2026.3.2, the `openclaw doctor` command does not auto-add the required `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` configuration for existing `loopback` deployments. This causes the Control UI to fail with "Origin localhost:18789 not allowed" even when accessing from the same machine.

- Install method: npm
- OS: Ubuntu (Linux)
- Gateway bind mode: loopback

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Have an existing OpenClaw installation < 2026.2.26
2. Update to 2026.3.2 via npm
3. Run `openclaw doctor` (no errors reported)
4. Try to access Control UI at http://localhost:18789
5. Error: "Origin localhost:18789 not allowed"

### Actual Behavior

The config key is missing after update, requiring manual intervention.

### Workaround

Manually add to `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json`:

```json
"gateway": {
  "controlUi": {
    "allowedOrigins": ["http://localhost:18789", "http://127.0.0.1:18789"]
  }
}
```

Then restart gateway.

- Introduced in v2026.2.26 according to code comments
